name: Oceanid
description: Oceanids are nymphs in Greek mythology, daughters of Oceanus and Tethys. Numbering three thousand according to Hesiod, they represent the various bodies of fresh water found on Earth, from rivers and springs to lakes and fountains. Unlike the male river gods (Potamoi), Oceanids are generally associated with specific places or bodies of water, acting as their protectors and personifications. They are often depicted as beautiful young women, graceful and benevolent, embodying the life-giving and nurturing qualities of water. While immortal, their influence is often localized to their particular water source. They are known for their gentle nature and association with fertility and healing. Some Oceanids, like Doris and Metis, held positions of significant importance, becoming mothers of important deities and figures in Greek myth. Their vast number reflects the Greeks' deep connection with and understanding of the natural world.
subtitle: Nymphs of Fresh Water
appearance: Oceanids are typically depicted as beautiful young women with flowing hair, often adorned with water lilies or reeds. Their skin is often described as being fair and luminous, reflecting the purity of the water they embody. They may also possess features that subtly reflect their specific water source, such as the color of the water or the presence of aquatic plants in their hair. Their clothing is usually light and flowing, often resembling shimmering water or sea foam.
habitat: Oceanids reside in the fresh water bodies they are associated with, including rivers, springs, lakes, fountains, and wells. They are often found in secluded grottoes or near waterfalls, places of natural beauty and tranquility.
behavior: Oceanids are generally benevolent creatures, known for their grace, beauty, and nurturing qualities. They are often helpful to mortals, offering guidance, healing, and protection. They are closely tied to their water source and will defend it against harm or pollution. While generally peaceful, they can be formidable when provoked, able to unleash the power of their water against those who threaten their domain.
weaknesses: Drying up of their water source

keyMyths.title: The Birth of Achilles
keyMyths.description: Thetis, an Oceanid, was destined to bear a son greater than his father. To prevent this, Zeus forced her to marry the mortal Peleus. Their son was Achilles, who she tried to make immortal by dipping him in the River Styx, holding him by his heel, leaving it vulnerable. This is the origin of Achilles' heel.

keyMyths.title: Metis and the Birth of Athena
keyMyths.description: Metis, an Oceanid known for her wisdom, was Zeus's first wife. A prophecy foretold that she would bear children wiser than Zeus, so he swallowed her whole. Later, Hephaestus split Zeus's head open, and Athena emerged, fully grown and armed, from Zeus's head. This story highlights Metis's incredible wisdom and influence despite being consumed by Zeus.

keyMyths.title: Doris and Her Children
keyMyths.description: Doris, another prominent Oceanid, married Nereus and bore fifty daughters, the Nereids. These sea nymphs are known for their beauty and helpfulness to sailors. Doris's role as the mother of the Nereids underscores the Oceanids' connection to the sea and their influence over marine affairs.

sources.author: Hesiod
sources.description: A foundational text of Greek mythology that provides a genealogy of the gods and goddesses, including the Oceanids.
sources.text: A foundational text of Greek mythology that provides a genealogy of the gods and goddesses, including the Oceanids.

sources.author: Apollodorus
sources.description: A comprehensive handbook of Greek mythology, offering accounts of various myths and legends associated with the Oceanids.
sources.text: A comprehensive handbook of Greek mythology, offering accounts of various myths and legends associated with the Oceanids.

sources.author: Robert Graves
sources.description: A popular retelling of Greek myths, providing a narrative and accessible account of the Oceanids and their role in various stories.
